project.name: TempoCode
project.core_idea: A learning app inspired by typing trainers like Monkeytype, but focused on programming fluency, program structure, and software modeling.
audience: learners practicing syntax, control flow, and problem solving

meaningful_conversations.summary: TempoCode is imagined as a minimalist practice environment where users respond to programming prompts by writing code or modeling program logic. The experience should feel focused, fast, and encouraging, with lightweight feedback after successful implementation.
important_decisions: The app should not just be a typing game; it should teach coding structure and reasoning.
The user experience should borrow the clean, minimal feel of Monkeytype.
Positive feedback should be simple and motivating, similar to a game-like reward loop.

meaningful_conversations.summary: The project direction settled around multiple modes of abstraction. Instead of only writing code, users could first model logic, then implement it.

modes.name: Flow Mode
modes.description: Users arrange control-flow shapes or logic blocks in the correct sequence before coding.

modes.name: Implementation Mode
modes.description: Users write code that matches the previously modeled flow.

modes.name: Structure / Modeling Mode
modes.description: Users practice designing program structure, possibly with UML-style or diagram-based tasks.

important_decisions: Flow Mode should connect directly to Implementation Mode.
The app can store a database of problems that support both diagrams and code answers.
The platform should help users move between abstraction levels.

meaningful_conversations.summary: TempoCode should maintain a database of coding and modeling problems. Problems may include prompts, expected control-flow structure, solution code, test cases, difficulty, and learning objectives.
The preferred stack moved toward a modern web application with a React frontend, a Python backend, and PostgreSQL for persistence.

frontend.purpose: Build the user interface for practice modes, problem pages, and feedback displays.

backend.purpose: Serve problems, handle submissions, connect to the database, and communicate with AI/LLM services.

database.purpose: Store users, problems, submissions, attempts, feedback, and possibly embeddings for AI-supported features.

meaningful_conversations.summary: The system may use both REST APIs and WebSockets. REST is suitable for normal page data, while WebSockets may be useful for live grading, real-time feedback, or interactive practice sessions.

PostgreSQL was discussed as more than a traditional relational database. Extensions such as vector search can support AI-heavy features by storing embeddings for semantic search, recommendation, or similarity matching.
architecture_takeaways: Modern AI apps often combine relational data with vector-style search.
PostgreSQL can support both normal structured tables and AI-adjacent features through extensions.
TempoCode could eventually use embeddings to recommend similar problems, search explanations, or compare user submissions conceptually.

meaningful_conversations.summary: The early schema should support users, coding problems, submissions, attempts, feedback, and progress tracking.

core_tables.name: submissions
core_tables.purpose: Stores user answers, code, diagrams, and timestamps.

core_tables.name: test_cases
core_tables.purpose: Stores input/output expectations for code problems.

core_tables.name: feedback
core_tables.purpose: Stores grading results, AI comments, or rule-based feedback.

core_tables.name: progress
core_tables.purpose: Tracks user skill growth, completed problems, streaks, and mode-specific progress.

meaningful_conversations.summary: TempoCode is being treated as a real software engineering project rather than just a coding experiment. Tasks are being organized in a Trello board, including code discovery and design research.
important_practices: Use code discovery to learn from existing apps like Monkeytype.
Break work into manageable project tasks.
Use diagrams to communicate the system design.

meaningful_conversations.summary: The user wanted to study how online typing applications and puzzle applications are structured in order to borrow useful design ideas without copying blindly.
questions_to_investigate: How are practice sessions represented in code?
How is timing or scoring handled?
How are problems loaded and rendered?
How does the UI stay minimal while still being useful?
How can flow diagrams be stored and compared?

possible_json_schema_for_problems.title: Find the Largest Number
possible_json_schema_for_problems.prompt: Write a program that takes two numbers and returns the larger one.
hints: Compare the first number to the second number.

open_questions: Should Flow Mode use drag-and-drop shapes, ordered cards, or both?
Should grading be rule-based first, AI-assisted later, or both from the beginning?
Should the first version focus only on Python problems?
Should users log in during the MVP, or should practice work without accounts first?
How much real-time feedback should WebSockets handle in the first version?

mvp_recommendation.goal: Build a small working version before adding heavy AI features.
recommended_scope: simple rule-based grading for a few Python problems
Flow Mode prototype using ordered steps/cards
